content: The fundamental frequency of a closed organ pipe is same as the first overtone frequency of an open organ pipe. If the length of open organ pipe is 50 cm , then the length of closed organ pipe is
explanation:
For closed organ pipe, fundamental frequency,

$$ f_1=\frac{v}{4 l_c} $$

For open organ pipe, first overtone frequency,

$$ f_2=\frac{2 v}{2 l_0}=\frac{v}{l_0} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Now, } f_1=f_2 \Rightarrow \frac{v}{4 l_c}=\frac{v}{l_0}\\ &\therefore l_c=\frac{l_0}{4}=\frac{50}{4}=12.5 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned} $$

content: A standing wave $y=A \sin \left(\frac{20}{3} \pi x\right) \cos (1000 \pi t)$ is maintained in a taught string, where $y$ and $x$ are in metres. The distance between two successive points oscillating with the amplitude $\frac{A}{2}$ across a node is
explanation:
The distance of a oscillating point from a node is

$$ y=A \sin \left(\frac{20}{3} \pi x\right) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { For } y=\frac{A}{2}, \sin \left(\frac{20}{3} \pi x\right)=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{20 \pi x}{3}=\frac{\pi}{6} \\ & \therefore \quad x=\frac{1}{40} \mathrm{~m}=\frac{100}{40} \mathrm{~cm}=2.5 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned} $$

∴ Distance between two successive oscillating points is

$$ D=2 x=2 \times 2.5=5 \mathrm{~cm} $$

content: The fundamental frequency of an open organ pipe is $$600 \mathrm{~Hz}$$. The first overtone of this pipe has same frequency as first overtone of a closed organ pipe. If speed of sound is $$330 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$, then the length of a closed organ pipe is
explanation:
To solve this problem, we need to understand the relationship between the frequencies of open and closed organ pipes and how their lengths are connected to these frequencies.

Firstly, let’s consider the open organ pipe. The fundamental frequency of an open organ pipe is given by:

$$ f_1 = \frac{v}{2L_1} $$

where $ f_1 = 600 \, \text{Hz} $ is the fundamental frequency, $ v = 330 \, \text{m/s} $ is the speed of sound, and $ L_1 $ is the length of the open organ pipe.

Hence:

$$ 600 = \frac{330}{2L_1} $$

Solving for $ L_1 $, we get:

$$ L_1 = \frac{330}{2 \times 600} = \frac{330}{1200} = 0.275 \, \text{m} = 27.5 \, \text{cm} $$

For the open organ pipe, the first overtone frequency will be:

$$ f_2 = \frac{2v}{2L_1} = \frac{v}{L_1} = \frac{330}{0.275} \, \text{m} = 1200 \, \text{Hz} $$

Now, considering the closed organ pipe, the first overtone frequency for the closed organ pipe (which corresponds to the third harmonic) is given by:

$$ f'_2 = \frac{3v}{4L_2} $$

We are given that the first overtone of the open organ pipe (which is $ 1200 \, \text{Hz} $) is equal to the first overtone of the closed organ pipe. Hence:

$$ 1200 = \frac{3 \times 330}{4L_2} $$

Solving for $ L_2 $, we get:

$$ 1200 = \frac{990}{4L_2} $$

Rearranging:

$$ 4L_2 = \frac{990}{1200} $$

$$ L_2 = \frac{990}{4800} \, \text{m} = 0.20625 \, \text{m} = 20.625 \, \text{cm} $$

Given the options, there appears to be a minor discrepancy. However, the closest value is:

Option A: 21 cm

content: The wavelength of two waves are 40 and $$42 \mathrm{~cm}$$ respectively. If the temperature of the room is $$20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ then what will be the number of beats produced per second by these waves. When the speed of sound at $$0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$$ is $$332 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$ ?
explanation:
Beats will be formed as the wavelengths (hence frequencies) are similar.

$$\begin{aligned} & \lambda_1=40 \mathrm{~cm} ~\lambda_2=42 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & v \propto \sqrt{T} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{273+20}{273}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad v_2=319.23 \\ & v_1=\frac{v_1}{\lambda_1}=\frac{319.23}{0.40}=798.075 \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & v_2=\frac{v_2}{\lambda_2}=\frac{319.23}{0.42}=760.071 \mathrm{~Hz} . \end{aligned}$$

No. of beats $$=v_2-v_1=798-760=38 \mathrm{~Hz}$$

content: When a string is divided into four segments of $$l_1, l_2, l_3$$ and $$l_4$$. The fundamental frequencies of these three segments are $$v_1, v_2, v_3$$ and $$v_4$$, respectively. The original fundamental frequency $$(v)$$ of the string is
explanation:
The fundamental frequency of a string is inversely proportional to its length. So, if the string is divided into four segments of lengths $$l_1, l_2, l_3$$ and $$l_4$$, then the fundamental frequencies of these segments will be inversely proportional to their lengths.

Let the original length of the string be $$L$$. Then, we have:

$$l_1 + l_2 + l_3 + l_4 = L$$

The fundamental frequency of the original string is given by:

$$v = \frac{1}{2L} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

where $$T$$ is the tension in the string and $$\mu$$ is the mass per unit length of the string.

The fundamental frequencies of the four segments are given by:

$$v_1 = \frac{1}{2l_1} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{1}{2l_2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

$$v_3 = \frac{1}{2l_3} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

$$v_4 = \frac{1}{2l_4} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

Adding these equations, we get:

$$v_1 + v_2 + v_3 + v_4 = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} \left(\frac{1}{l_1} + \frac{1}{l_2} + \frac{1}{l_3} + \frac{1}{l_4} \right)$$

Now, using the equation $$l_1 + l_2 + l_3 + l_4 = L$$, we can write:

$$\frac{1}{l_1} + \frac{1}{l_2} + \frac{1}{l_3} + \frac{1}{l_4} = \frac{L}{l_1 l_2 l_3 l_4}$$

Substituting this into the previous equation, we get:

$$v_1 + v_2 + v_3 + v_4 = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} \frac{L}{l_1 l_2 l_3 l_4}$$

Since $$v = \frac{1}{2L} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$, we can write:

$$v_1 + v_2 + v_3 + v_4 = \frac{vL}{l_1 l_2 l_3 l_4}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{v_1} + \frac{1}{v_2} + \frac{1}{v_3} + \frac{1}{v_4}}$$

This is option C.

content: In a resonance coloum first and second resonance are obtained at depths 24 cm and 78 cm the third resonance will be obtained at depth.
explanation:
Given, first resonance column at depth, $${l_1} = 24$$ cm

Second resonance column at depth, $${l_2} = 78$$ cm

Third resonance column at depth, $${l_3} = ?$$

As

$${l_1} + x = {\lambda \over 4} = 24$$ ..... (i)

$${l_2} + x = {{3\lambda } \over 4} = 78$$ ........ (ii)

$${l_3} + x = {{5\lambda } \over 4}$$ ........ (iii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii),

$$x = {{{l_2} - 3{l_1}} \over 2} = {{78 - 3(24)} \over 2} = 3$$ ....... (iv)

From Eqs. (iii) and (i), $${{{l_3} + x} \over {{l_1} + x}} = 5$$

$${l_3} = 5{l_1} + 5x - x = 5{l_1} + 4x$$

Substituting $$l_1$$ and x value, we get

$${l_3} = 5(24) + 4(3) \Rightarrow {l_3} = 132$$ cm.

content:
A submarine A travelling at 17 m/s is being chased along the line of its velocity by another submarine B travelling at 34 m/s. B sends a sonar signal of 600 Hz to detect A and receives a reflected sound of frequency $$\nu$$. The of $$\nu$$ is

[Speed of sound in water = 1500 ms$$-$$1]
explanation:
Given, velocity of submarine A, vA = 17 m/s

Velocity of submarine B, vB = 34 m/s

Signal sent by submarine B is detected by submarine A can be shown as

Frequency of signal, f0 = 600 Hz

Speed of sound in water vs = 1500 ms$$-$$1

Frequency received by submarine A is

$${f_1} = \left( {{{{v_s} - {v_A}} \over {{v_s} - {v_B}}}} \right){f_0} = \left[ {{{1500 - 17} \over {1500 - 34}}} \right] \times 600$$ Hz

$${f_1} = {{1483} \over {1466}} \times 600$$ ..... (i)

Frequency received by submarine B is

$${f_2} = \left( {{{{v_s} + {v_B}} \over {{v_s} + {v_A}}}} \right){f_1}$$

Substituting given values and f1 value from Eq. (i), we get

$${f_2} = \left( {{{1500 + 34} \over {1500 + 17}}} \right) \times \left[ {{{1483} \over {1466}} \times 600} \right]$$

$${f_2} = 1.0112 \times 1.0115 \times 600$$

$${f_2} = 613.7$$ Hz.

content: Transverse waves of the same frequency are generated in two steel wires A and B. The diameter of A is twice that of B and the tension in A is half that in B. The ratio of the velocities of the waves in A and B is
explanation:
The velocity of transverse waves is given by $$v = \sqrt {{T \over m}} $$ where, T = Tension and m = mass per unit length of the wire. If r is the radius of the wire and $$\rho$$ its density then,

$$m = \pi {r^2}\rho $$

$$\therefore$$ $$v = \sqrt {{T \over m}} = \sqrt {{T \over {\pi {r^2}\rho }}} $$

$$\therefore$$ $${v_A} = {{\sqrt {{T_A}} } \over {{r_A}\sqrt {\pi \rho } }}$$ and

$${v_B} = {{\sqrt {{T_B}} } \over {{r_B}\sqrt {\pi \rho } }}$$

Now, $${{{v_A}} \over {{v_B}}} = \sqrt {{{{T_A}} \over {{T_B}}}} .\,{{{r_B}} \over {{r_A}}}$$

$$\because$$ $${r_A} = 2{r_B}$$ and $${T_A} = {1 \over 2}{T_B}$$

Hence, $${{{v_A}} \over {{v_B}}} = {1 \over {2\sqrt 2 }}$$

content: In the diagram shown below, both the strings AB and CD are made of same material and have same cross-section. The pulleys are light and fictionless. If the speed of wave in string AB is v1 and in CD is v2, then $${{{v_1}} \over {{v_2}}}$$ is
explanation:
Let the speed of wave in string be v and tension in string be T.

$$v \propto \sqrt T $$

$${{{v_{AB}}} \over {{v_{CD}}}} = \sqrt {{T \over {2T}}} = {1 \over {\sqrt 2 }}$$

content: The equation of progressive wave is given by $$Y = \sin \left[ {x\left( {{t \over 5} - {x \over 9}} \right) + {\pi \over 6}} \right]$$ cm. Which one of the following is correct?
explanation:
Given,

$$Y = \sin \left[ {x\left( {{t \over 5} - {x \over 9}} \right) + {\pi \over 6}} \right]$$ ...... (i)

The general equation of progressive wave is

$$Y = A\sin \left[ {2\pi \left( {{t \over T} - {x \over \lambda }} \right) + \phi } \right]$$ ...... (ii)

On comparing Eq. (i) with Eq. (ii), we get

A = 1 cm, f = 0.1 Hz, v = 1.8 cm/s and $${{2\pi } \over \lambda } = {\pi \over 9}$$

$$\Rightarrow$$ $$\lambda$$ = 18 cm

content:
Two cars approach a stationary observer from opposite sides as shown in the figure.

The observer hears no beats. If the frequency of the horn of the car B is 504 Hz, then the frequency of the horn of the car A will be
explanation:
Apparent frequency of car A = Apparent frequency of car B

$$ \Rightarrow {v \over {v - {v_s}}} imes u = {v \over {v - v{'_s}}} imes u '$$

$$ \Rightarrow { u \over {v - 15}} = {{504} \over {v - 30}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow u = {{340 - 15} \over {340 - 30}} imes 504$$ [$$ ecause$$ velocity of sound in air is 340 m/s]

$$ \Rightarrow u = 529.2$$ Hz

content:
The vibrations of a string of length 60 cm fixed at both ends are represented by the equation

$$y = 4\sin \left( {{{\pi x} \over {15}}} \right)\cos (96\pi t)$$

where x and y are in cm and t is in second. Calculate the velocity of the particle at x = 7.5 cm at t = 0.25 s.
explanation:
The given equation is

$$y = 4\sin \left( {{{\pi x} \over {15}}} \right)\cos (96\pi t)$$ ...... (i)

This equation is similar to the displacement equation of a travelling wave, $$y = A\sin (\omega t - kx)$$

On comparing these two equations, we get

$$\omega = 2\pi v = 96\pi $$

or, $$\nu = {{96\pi } \over {2\pi }} = 48$$ Hz

Now, the velocity of the string at a point x at time t is obtained by differentiating Eq. (i) w.r.t.

$$\therefore$$ $$v = {{dy} \over {dt}} = - (4 \times 9\pi ) \times \sin \left( {{{\pi x} \over {15}}} \right)\sin (96\pi t)$$

At x = 7.5 cm = 7.5 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$2 m and t = 0.25 s,

$$v = - 384\pi \sin \left( {\pi \times {{7.5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}} \over {15}}} \right)\sin \left( {96\pi \times {{25} \over {100}}} \right)$$

$$ = - 384\pi \sin \left( {{\pi \over {200}}} \right)\sin (24\pi )$$

$$ = 0$$ ($$\because$$ $$\sin 24\pi = 0$$)

Hence, the velocity of the particle is zero.
